mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno (Policy Engine)
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that allows users to manage security, compliance, and governance through policy-as-code. It is particularly useful for teams looking to enforce policies without needing to learn a new language. Key trade-offs include the need for proper policy management and potential performance impacts if overly complex policies are applied.

steps.title: Add the Kyverno Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Kyverno Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno

```

> This is the official repository for Kyverno.

steps.title: Install Kyverno CRDs
steps.description:
This step installs the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) required for Kyverno to function properly.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kyverno/kyverno/v1.17.1/config/release/kyverno-crds.yaml

```

> This command fetches and applies the CRDs from the specified version.

steps.title: Install Kyverno using Helm
steps.description:
This step installs the Kyverno policy engine in the kube-system namespace using Helm.

```bash

helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kube-system --create-namespace --version 1.17.1

```

> This installs Kyverno with the specified version.

steps.title: Verify Kyverno installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Kyverno pods are running and ready in the kube-system namespace.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=kyverno

```

> You should see the Kyverno pods in a Running state.

steps.title: Create a sample policy
steps.description:
This step creates a sample policy to demonstrate Kyverno's functionality.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

```

> This policy requires the 'app' label on all Pods.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

steps.title: Verify the policy is applied
steps.description:
This step checks if the policy is enforced by creating a Pod without the required label.

```bash

kubectl run test-pod --image=nginx --restart=Never

```

> This should fail due to the missing 'app' label.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Kyverno
uninstall.description:
This step removes Kyverno and its associated resources. Be aware that any policies created will be lost.

```bash

helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kube-system

kubectl delete -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kyverno/kyverno/v1.17.1/config/release/kyverno-crds.yaml

```

> This command uninstalls Kyverno from the kube-system namespace.
> This command removes the CRDs associated with Kyverno.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kyverno
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your policies. This step will upgrade Kyverno to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get clusterpolicies -o yaml > backup-policies.yaml

helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kube-system --version 1.17.1

kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=kyverno

```

> Backup existing policies.
> This command upgrades Kyverno to the specified version.
> Verify that the Kyverno pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'validation failed'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that a policy is preventing the creation of a resource due to missing required fields.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the resource being created meets the requirements specified in the policies.

troubleshooting.title: Kyverno pods are in 'CrashLoopBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if there is a misconfiguration in the policies or CRDs.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Kyverno pods using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kube-system' for more details.

troubleshooting.title: Policy not enforcing as expected
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The policy may not be applied correctly or is not matching the resources.

**Fix:**
Verify the policy definition and ensure it is applied using 'kubectl get clusterpolicies'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'unable to recognize' for CRD
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the CRD is not installed or is not recognized by the cluster.

**Fix:**
Reapply the CRDs using the command: 'kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kyverno/kyverno/v1.17.1/config/release/kyverno-crds.yaml'.

versionNotes.changes: Improved performance for policy validation, added support for new Kubernetes resource types.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kyverno pods running in the 'Running' state and the sample policy enforced correctly when creating Pods.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the required tools installed.
